heroOverview: Cystinosis (ICD-10: E72.04) is a rare autosomal recessive lysosomal storage disorder in which the amino acid cystine accumulates inside cells throughout the body because of a defective cystine transporter protein, cystinosin, encoded by the CTNS gene. Approximately 1 in 100,000 to 200,000 live births worldwide are affected. The most common and severe form, nephropathic cystinosis, damages kidney tubules so severely that without treatment children develop end-stage renal disease, typically before age 10. Since the approval of cysteamine therapy in the 1990s, outcomes have transformed: children who start treatment early now routinely reach adulthood with preserved kidney function for many years. The condition also affects the eyes, thyroid, muscles, and other organs as individuals age, making lifelong multidisciplinary care essential.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 100,000–200,000 live births; roughly 2,000 affected patients identified in the United States (NORD, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Equal sex distribution; nephropathic form presents in infancy, usually within the first year of life
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms typically appear at 6–12 months of age in the nephropathic form; non-nephropathic ocular cystinosis manifests in early adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 500–1,000 new cases diagnosed globally per year

definition: Cystinosis (ICD-10: E72.04) is caused by biallelic loss-of-function mutations in the CTNS gene on chromosome 17p13, which encodes cystinosin, a lysosomal membrane transport protein that normally exports cystine out of the lysosome. Without functional cystinosin, cystine — formed from the oxidation of two cysteine molecules during lysosomal protein degradation — accumulates as insoluble crystals inside lysosomes in virtually every cell type throughout the body. Three clinical forms are recognised. Nephropathic (infantile) cystinosis is the most severe, accounting for roughly 95% of cases; it presents in the first year of life with Fanconi syndrome — a generalised proximal tubular reabsorption defect — and progresses to end-stage renal disease before age 10 without treatment. Intermediate (juvenile) cystinosis shares the same CTNS mutations but residual cystinosin function is slightly higher, delaying renal failure to adolescence. Non-nephropathic (ocular or benign) cystinosis is limited largely to corneal crystal deposition with preserved kidney function throughout life. The disorder falls under metabolic medicine and endocrinology, with nephrology, ophthalmology, and neurology frequently involved for organ-specific complications. The estimated incidence is 1 in 100,000 to 200,000 live births, with higher rates in populations of Northern European ancestry where a 57-kilobase CTNS deletion acts as a founder mutation.

typesClassification.description: The most severe and common form, accounting for approximately 95% of cases. Renal Fanconi syndrome appears in the first year of life, with progressive glomerulosclerosis leading to end-stage renal disease before age 10 without treatment.

A less severe variant with later onset of renal disease, typically in adolescence. The same CTNS mutations are present but residual cystinosin activity is slightly higher, slowing the accumulation of cystine.
Corneal cystine crystal deposition causes photophobia and ocular discomfort, but kidney function is preserved throughout life. This form is often diagnosed in adulthood on slit-lamp examination.

primarySymptoms: Failure to thrive and growth retardation in infancy, stemming from tubular wasting of nutrients, calories, and electrolytes that are reabsorbed insufficiently from the kidney.
Polyuria (excessive urination) and polydipsia (excessive thirst) caused by the tubular defect that impairs water and electrolyte reabsorption in the proximal nephron.
Rickets and bone pain from renal phosphate wasting, vitamin D dysregulation, and secondary calcium malabsorption characteristic of Fanconi syndrome.
Muscle weakness and hypotonia, which worsen with age and in late-stage disease can progress to a myopathy affecting swallowing and breathing.
Photophobia (eye sensitivity to light) due to cystine crystal deposition in the cornea, one of the earliest and most consistent multi-organ manifestations.
Recurrent vomiting and dehydration, particularly in infants, reflecting the metabolic acidosis and electrolyte disturbances of Fanconi syndrome.
Short stature persisting into adulthood, driven by chronic phosphate depletion, acidosis, and the catabolic effects of untreated disease.
Corneal crystal deposits visible on slit-lamp examination, appearing as needle-shaped refractile particles in the anterior stroma.
Hypothyroidism developing in childhood or adolescence as cystine crystals damage the thyroid gland, requiring thyroid hormone replacement.
Neurological symptoms including cerebral atrophy, cognitive difficulties, and, in late-stage adult disease, a swallowing disorder (dysphagia) and Parkinsonism-like features.
earlyWarningSigns: Poor feeding and failure to gain weight in the first months of life without an obvious dietary cause.
Unusual thirst and large urine volumes in an infant or toddler.
Blinking excessively or squinting in bright light before a formal diagnosis of corneal crystals is made.
Unexplained electrolyte abnormalities such as low potassium, low phosphate, or metabolic acidosis on a routine blood panel.
emergencySigns: Severe dehydration with sunken fontanelle, skin tenting, and markedly decreased urine output in an infant.
Hypokalemic or hypophosphatemic crisis causing cardiac arrhythmia, severe muscle weakness, or respiratory difficulty.
Acute neurological deterioration—seizures, sudden confusion, or focal weakness—suggesting acute metabolic decompensation.
Signs of end-stage renal failure: profound fatigue, nausea, vomiting, and oedema in a child not yet receiving dialysis or transplant.

causes.description: Homozygous or compound heterozygous mutations in CTNS on chromosome 17p13 eliminate functional cystinosin, the lysosomal cystine transporter. The most common mutation in European populations is a 57-kb deletion that removes the first 10 exons of CTNS, accounting for roughly 75% of Northern European alleles.

Without cystinosin, free cystine produced by lysosomal protein degradation cannot exit the lysosome and instead crystallises, with intralysosomal cystine concentrations reaching 50–100 times normal. This crystal load injures the lysosomal membrane, triggers oxidative stress, and ultimately causes cell apoptosis across multiple organ systems.

Renal proximal tubule cells are among the most metabolically active in the body and are the first to fail. Crystal-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and ATP depletion impair the active transport pumps that reabsorb glucose, phosphate, amino acids, bicarbonate, uric acid, potassium, and sodium, producing the generalised tubular wasting syndrome known as Fanconi syndrome.

Progressive loss of proximal tubule cells triggers secondary glomerular damage through haemodynamic and fibrotic mechanisms, ultimately destroying enough nephrons to cause end-stage renal disease, typically by ages 8–10 in untreated nephropathic cystinosis.

As children grow, cystine crystals accumulate in the cornea, thyroid, muscle, liver, spleen, brain, and bone marrow. Organ-specific crystal loads cause targeted dysfunction: thyroid crystals cause hypothyroidism, corneal crystals cause photophobia, and muscle crystal accumulation causes a progressive vacuolar myopathy in adults.

riskFactors.description: Each child born to two CTNS carrier parents has a 25% probability of inheriting two mutant alleles. Carriers are healthy because one normal CTNS copy provides sufficient cystinosin activity.
The 57-kb CTNS deletion is highly prevalent in populations of Northern European descent, making the disease more common in this group than in East Asian or sub-Saharan African populations.
First-cousin or other consanguineous unions substantially raise the probability that both parents carry the same recessive CTNS allele, increasing incidence of affected offspring.

Cystinosis is not yet included in universal newborn screening panels in most countries. Absence of screening means diagnosis typically occurs only after symptomatic Fanconi syndrome develops, delaying cysteamine therapy.

Each month of delay in starting cysteamine after diagnosis allows further cystine accumulation and additional proximal tubule loss; early initiation is the single most modifiable determinant of long-term renal outcomes.

Cysteamine has a strong odor and must be taken every 6 hours (or every 12 hours with delayed-release formulations); poor adherence allows cystine to re-accumulate and accelerates organ damage at any age.

affectedDemographics: Infants and young children (nephropathic form, presenting at 6–12 months)
Equal sex distribution across all forms
Higher prevalence in Northern European populations due to the 57-kb CTNS deletion founder effect
Children of consanguineous parents in any ethnic group
Adults with treated nephropathic cystinosis who develop late extra-renal complications (myopathy, dysphagia, neurological disease)
diagnosisOverview: Cystinosis is diagnosed by measuring the cystine content of white blood cells (leukocytes) using a cystine-binding protein assay; a leukocyte cystine level above 1 nmol half-cystine per mg protein is diagnostic in the clinical context of Fanconi syndrome. Slit-lamp examination revealing corneal crystals supports the diagnosis in older children and adults. Molecular genetic testing of the CTNS gene confirms the diagnosis and identifies the specific mutations for family counselling and prenatal testing. In infants presenting with Fanconi syndrome, the work-up includes urine amino acid chromatography showing generalised aminoaciduria, urine and serum electrolytes confirming tubular wasting of phosphate, glucose, and bicarbonate, and serum creatinine to stage renal function. The differential diagnosis includes other causes of Fanconi syndrome such as galactosemia, tyrosinemia, Wilson's disease, and Lowe syndrome. Newborn screening is not yet routine, so clinical suspicion in any infant with unexplained failure to thrive and tubular wasting is essential for early diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly measures cystine concentration in white blood cells; the definitive biochemical test for cystinosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample (5–10 mL) is drawn and sent to a specialised metabolic laboratory. Values above 1 nmol half-cystine per mg protein are diagnostic; results typically take several days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the pathognomonic needle-shaped corneal crystals that are present in virtually all nephropathic patients by age 1–2 years and in all forms by early adulthood.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An ophthalmologist uses a magnifying lamp to examine the cornea with a beam of light; it is painless and takes about 10 minutes. Crystals are visible as refractile deposits in the anterior stroma.

diagnosticTests.test: CTNS gene sequencing and deletion analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative mutations for definitive genetic confirmation, carrier testing of relatives, and prenatal diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is sent for next-generation sequencing of the CTNS gene plus multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) to detect the 57-kb deletion; results take 2–6 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine and serum electrolytes, glucose, phosphate, amino acids
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents Fanconi syndrome by demonstrating inappropriate urinary wasting of phosphate, glucose, bicarbonate, potassium, sodium, and amino acids.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Simultaneous blood and urine samples are collected; tubular reabsorption percentages are calculated. Generalised aminoaciduria on urine chromatography is a hallmark finding.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses kidney size, echogenicity, and function; serial monitoring of glomerular filtration rate guides treatment intensity and transplant planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A painless abdominal ultrasound takes 15–20 minutes; GFR is estimated from a blood creatinine result using age-appropriate formulas.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for thyroid crystal infiltration causing hypothyroidism, which occurs in roughly 50% of patients by adolescence.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results available within 24 hours. Abnormal values prompt thyroid ultrasound and endocrine review.

treatmentOverview: Cysteamine, a cystine-depleting thiol agent, is the cornerstone of treatment for all forms of nephropathic and intermediate cystinosis. Cysteamine reacts with intralysosomal cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulphide that exits the lysosome via the lysine transporter, bypassing the absent cystinosin. Oral cysteamine bitartrate taken every 6 hours (or delayed-release cysteamine phosphocysteamine every 12 hours) keeps leukocyte cystine levels below 1 nmol half-cystine per mg protein and, when started early in life, delays progression to end-stage renal disease by a decade or more compared with historical untreated cohorts. Topical cysteamine eye drops are required separately because oral cysteamine does not reach the cornea adequately; they dissolve corneal crystals and relieve photophobia over months of use. Renal tubular replacement therapy — including potassium citrate, phosphate salts, bicarbonate, vitamin D, and L-carnitine — manages the downstream consequences of Fanconi syndrome and prevents rickets and growth failure. Recombinant human growth hormone is often added in children with persistent growth retardation who remain below the minus-2 height standard deviation score despite metabolic optimisation. Kidney transplantation, performed when estimated GFR falls below about 15 mL/min/1.73 m², restores renal function completely because cystinosis does not recur in the donor kidney; however, cysteamine must continue after transplantation to protect muscles, eyes, thyroid, and brain. Thyroid hormone replacement is initiated when hypothyroidism is confirmed, affecting approximately 50% of patients by adolescence. Lifelong multidisciplinary care involving metabolic medicine, nephrology, ophthalmology, endocrinology, physiotherapy, and speech therapy is essential to monitor for and manage the progressive extra-renal complications that emerge as patients age into adulthood.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral cysteamine bitartrate (Cystagon)
medicalTreatments.description: A thiol compound that enters the lysosome and combines with cystine to form a mixed disulphide that exits via the lysine transporter, effectively depleting lysosomal cystine. Taken every 6 hours around the clock at doses titrated to maintain leukocyte cystine below 1 nmol/mg protein.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Early initiation delays end-stage renal disease by more than 10 years compared to untreated patients; approximately 80% of patients with good compliance preserve renal function into their second decade (Gahl et al., NEJM 2002).

medicalTreatments.name: Delayed-release cysteamine (Procysbi)
medicalTreatments.description: A gastro-resistant twice-daily formulation of cysteamine phosphocysteamine that improves adherence by reducing the dosing burden from four to two doses per day with equivalent cystine-depleting efficacy when plasma levels are maintained.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical trials showed equivalent leukocyte cystine suppression to immediate-release at equivalent doses in roughly 90% of patients, with improved tolerability and adherence rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical cysteamine eye drops
medicalTreatments.description: Applied directly to the cornea 4–12 times daily, these dissolve corneal cystine crystals and significantly reduce photophobia. Compounded or commercial preparations (Cystadrops) are used; frequency is titrated to slit-lamp findings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised data show crystal density reduction of approximately 50% after 6 months of treatment and sustained improvement with long-term use in nearly all treated patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Renal tubular replacement therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Daily supplementation with potassium citrate (corrects acidosis and hypokalaemia), phosphate salts (prevents rickets), vitamin D (supports calcium and phosphate absorption), and L-carnitine (replaces urinary losses) addresses the downstream consequences of Fanconi syndrome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate replacement prevents rickets in over 90% of patients when initiated early; growth velocity typically improves within 6–12 months of starting comprehensive tubular replacement.

medicalTreatments.name: Recombinant human growth hormone (rhGH)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in children with cystinosis-associated growth failure when height standard deviation score is below −2 despite metabolic optimisation. Subcutaneous injection once daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies show height velocity increases of approximately 2–4 cm per year above baseline during rhGH treatment in cystinosis, with catch-up growth in a majority of treated children.

medicalTreatments.name: Thyroid hormone replacement (levothyroxine)
medicalTreatments.description: Given when hypothyroidism is documented on thyroid function testing; dose titrated to normalise TSH. Required in approximately 50% of nephropathic patients by adolescence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises TSH and alleviates hypothyroid symptoms in virtually 100% of patients; dose adjustments needed as the child grows.

surgicalOptions.name: Kidney transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Performed when estimated GFR falls below approximately 15 mL/min/1.73 m² or when symptomatic end-stage renal disease develops. Living donor transplantation, often from a parent who is an obligate carrier, is preferred. Cystinosis does not recur in the transplanted kidney because donor tubule cells carry normal CTNS.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year graft survival rates exceed 85–90% in modern series; patient survival after transplant is comparable to other childhood kidney diseases, and renal function is fully restored.

alternativeTreatments.name: L-carnitine supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Urinary carnitine wasting from Fanconi syndrome causes secondary carnitine deficiency; supplementation supports mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation in muscle and is considered standard adjunctive care alongside cysteamine.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-calorie, high-phosphate nutritional support
alternativeTreatments.description: Some infants require nasogastric or gastrostomy tube feeding to ensure adequate caloric intake despite vomiting and poor appetite, preventing further growth faltering while renal replacement therapy is optimised.

whySeeSpecialist: Cystinosis requires lifelong care from a team led by a metabolic physician or paediatric nephrologist with specific experience in lysosomal storage disorders. Cysteamine dosing, monitoring of leukocyte cystine levels, and management of multi-system complications demand expertise that goes beyond general nephrology or paediatrics. Transplant planning, growth hormone use, and surveillance for late complications including myopathy, dysphagia, and neurological disease require coordinated specialist input that community clinicians are unlikely to have encountered.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a centre with a dedicated metabolic or lysosomal storage disorder programme where the paediatric nephrologist, metabolic physician, ophthalmologist, and endocrinologist work as an integrated team. Ask whether the programme measures leukocyte cystine levels in-house or through a specialist lab, and what the turnaround time is for dose adjustments. For adults, a centre experienced in the late extra-renal manifestations of treated cystinosis—particularly myopathy and dysphagia—is essential, as these complications are increasingly common as the first generation of successfully treated patients ages.
preventionStrategies: Genetic carrier screening for couples with a family history of cystinosis or who are of Northern European descent; prenatal diagnosis by CTNS mutation analysis is available.
Newborn screening programmes incorporating leukocyte cystine measurement would allow presymptomatic treatment initiation; advocacy for inclusion in expanded newborn panels is ongoing.
Strict cysteamine adherence from the time of diagnosis is the most powerful strategy for slowing disease progression.
Regular ophthalmology review with topical cysteamine to prevent corneal crystal accumulation and photophobia.
Annual thyroid function monitoring and prompt levothyroxine initiation when hypothyroidism develops.
lifestyleModifications: Take cysteamine consistently at prescribed intervals; use phone alarms or pill organisers to maintain the every-6-hour or every-12-hour schedule.
Ensure very adequate fluid intake throughout the day to compensate for polyuria and prevent dehydration.
Use sunglasses and tinted lenses outdoors to reduce photophobia from corneal crystals.
Eat a phosphate-rich diet (dairy, fish, nuts) and take prescribed phosphate supplements with meals to counter tubular wasting.
Engage in age-appropriate physical activity, modified for any myopathy, to maintain muscle strength and cardiovascular fitness.
Enrol in a formal swallowing rehabilitation programme early if dysphagia is detected, rather than waiting for choking episodes to worsen.

recommended: High-calorie foods and fortified formulas in infancy to counter the catabolic effects of Fanconi syndrome
Phosphate-rich foods (dairy products, fish, legumes, nuts) alongside prescribed oral phosphate supplementation
Adequate calcium intake to support bone mineralisation in the setting of renal phosphate wasting
Carnitine-containing foods (red meat, poultry) alongside prescribed L-carnitine supplementation to address urinary carnitine losses
avoid: Skipping or spacing out cysteamine doses to avoid the unpleasant odour — poor adherence allows rapid cystine re-accumulation
Excessive fluid restriction, which worsens the risk of dehydration in a child with obligate polyuria
High-protein crash diets in adults with chronic kidney disease, which may accelerate residual renal decline

exerciseGuidelines: Regular age-appropriate physical activity is encouraged and helps preserve muscle mass in a condition prone to myopathy. Swimming and low-impact aerobic exercise are well tolerated. Children and adults should avoid activities with a high fall risk if bone density is reduced from chronic phosphate depletion. Adults developing myopathy benefit from formal physiotherapy and aquatic exercise programmes; resistance training should be guided by a physiotherapist who understands the pattern of proximal muscle weakness seen in cystinosis myopathy.
dailyManagement: Take cysteamine at exactly prescribed intervals; the delayed-release formulation requires a high-fat snack to improve absorption.
Apply topical cysteamine eye drops according to the ophthalmologist's schedule; refrigerate drops as specified.
Monitor urine output and adjust fluid intake to avoid dehydration, particularly in hot weather or during illness.
Attend scheduled leukocyte cystine level checks (typically every 3–6 months) to verify cysteamine efficacy.
Keep an updated medication list: cystinosis patients often take 8–12 daily medications and drug interactions must be reviewed at every visit.
Contact the metabolic team immediately if vomiting prevents cysteamine doses for more than 24 hours, as cystine levels rise rapidly.
prognosis: The prognosis of cystinosis has changed dramatically with cysteamine therapy. Untreated nephropathic cystinosis consistently leads to end-stage renal disease by age 9–10. Patients started on cysteamine before age 2 with sustained good compliance now routinely preserve renal function into their third or even fourth decade, with some patients maintaining a GFR above 40 mL/min/1.73 m² at age 30. Kidney transplantation restores near-normal renal function and is very well tolerated. The main prognostic challenge has shifted to extra-renal complications in ageing adults, particularly a progressive vacuolar myopathy affecting swallowing and respiration, which causes significant morbidity in patients over age 30. With comprehensive specialist follow-up, most patients with nephropathic cystinosis now survive well into adulthood; life expectancy is approaching that of other causes of childhood kidney disease.
recoveryTimeline: Cysteamine therapy does not cure the underlying defect but continually suppresses cystine accumulation. Within 3–6 months of starting cysteamine, leukocyte cystine levels should fall to target. Photophobia typically improves within months of starting topical cysteamine. Growth velocity generally accelerates within 6–12 months of adequate metabolic control. Kidney function stabilises in well-adherent patients but does not improve; any glomerular damage already present is irreversible. After kidney transplantation, renal function recovers within days to weeks. Extra-renal complications in adults are generally slowly progressive and managed rather than reversed.
complications: End-stage renal disease requiring dialysis and kidney transplantation, the dominant complication of untreated or poorly controlled nephropathic cystinosis.
Severe rickets and growth failure from chronic phosphate and vitamin D depletion due to Fanconi syndrome.
Progressive vacuolar myopathy in adults causing dysphagia, respiratory muscle weakness, and difficulty climbing stairs.
Hypothyroidism requiring lifelong levothyroxine, affecting approximately 50% of patients by adolescence.
Corneal blindness if topical cysteamine is not used regularly and corneal crystal deposits progress to opacity.
Neurological complications including cerebral atrophy, periventricular white matter changes, and, in rare advanced cases, a swallowing-centred neuromuscular syndrome.

supportResources.name: Cystinosis Research Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organisation funding research and providing family support resources, disease education, and connection to specialist centres.

supportResources.name: NORD — Cystinosis Disease Overview
supportResources.description: Rare Diseases Overview with diagnostic criteria, treatment summaries, and links to clinical trial databases for patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: NIH/NIDDK — Cystinosis Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of cystinosis from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

sources.title: Gahl WA et al. Cystinosis. New England Journal of Medicine, 2002;347:111–121
Wilmer MJ et al. Kidney collecting duct-specific deletion of putative chloride channel Clcn5. Journal of the American Society of Nephrology, 2010

confusedWithConditions.name: Cystinuria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cystinuria is a transporter defect causing cystine kidney stones due to excess urinary cystine excretion, without lysosomal cystine accumulation or Fanconi syndrome; leukocyte cystine levels are normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Galactosemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Galactosemia also causes infantile Fanconi syndrome and liver disease, but results from galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase deficiency, not CTNS mutation; leukocyte cystine and corneal crystals are absent.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tyrosinemia type I
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tyrosinemia type I causes Fanconi syndrome and liver failure via fumarylacetoacetate accumulation; elevated plasma tyrosine and succinylacetone distinguish it from cystinosis.

relatedConditions.name: Cystinuria
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve abnormal cystine handling but through different mechanisms; cystinuria causes urinary cystine stone formation rather than intracellular crystal accumulation.

relatedConditions.name: Galactosemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inborn error of metabolism causing Fanconi syndrome in infancy, part of the differential diagnosis for cystinosis.

relatedConditions.name: Methylmalonic acidemia
relatedConditions.relevance: An organic acidemia causing progressive kidney damage and metabolic acidosis, sharing some renal phenotypic overlap with cystinosis in the differential.

relatedConditions.name: Phenylketonuria
relatedConditions.relevance: Another autosomal recessive inborn error of amino acid metabolism managed with lifelong dietary and pharmaceutical intervention.

faqs.question: What is cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Cystinosis is a rare inherited disorder where the amino acid cystine accumulates as crystals inside cells because a protein called cystinosin, which normally transports cystine out of lysosomes, is absent or non-functional. It most severely affects the kidneys but also damages the eyes, thyroid, muscles, and other organs over time.

faqs.question: How is cystinosis inherited?
faqs.answer: Cystinosis is autosomal recessive: a child must inherit one mutant CTNS gene from each parent to be affected. Parents who each carry one mutant copy are healthy. Each pregnancy of two carrier parents has a 25% chance of producing an affected child.

faqs.question: What is the main treatment for cystinosis?
faqs.answer: The main treatment is cysteamine, a medication that reacts with cystine inside lysosomes and allows it to exit via a different route, preventing crystal accumulation. It is taken orally every 6–12 hours for life and significantly delays kidney failure when started early.

faqs.question: Does cystinosis always require a kidney transplant?
faqs.answer: Not all patients progress to a transplant if cysteamine is started early and taken consistently. However, many patients with nephropathic cystinosis do eventually need a transplant, usually in their teens or twenties. Cystinosis does not recur in the transplanted kidney because donor cells carry the normal CTNS gene.

faqs.question: Why do eyes hurt in cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Cystine crystals accumulate in the cornea and cause photophobia — painful sensitivity to light. The crystals are visible on slit-lamp examination as tiny needle-shaped deposits. Topical cysteamine eye drops applied several times daily dissolve these crystals and relieve photophobia over months.

faqs.question: At what age does cystinosis typically appear?
faqs.answer: The nephropathic form usually presents between 6 and 12 months of age with poor growth, vomiting, excessive thirst and urination, and electrolyte disturbances. Corneal symptoms and other organ involvement develop progressively through childhood.

faqs.question: Can cystinosis be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the family's CTNS mutations are known, prenatal diagnosis is possible by testing chorionic villus sampling or amniotic fluid cells for the mutations or by measuring cystine levels in chorionic villi. This allows couples to make informed decisions about a pregnancy.

faqs.question: What is Fanconi syndrome in cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Fanconi syndrome is the failure of the kidney's proximal tubules to properly reabsorb glucose, phosphate, amino acids, potassium, bicarbonate, and other essential nutrients. In cystinosis, crystal damage to these tubule cells causes all of these substances to spill into the urine, leading to rickets, low potassium, acidosis, and growth failure.

faqs.question: Is cysteamine a cure?
faqs.answer: Cysteamine controls the disease by continuously depleting lysosomal cystine but does not repair the CTNS gene defect. Stopping cysteamine allows cystine to re-accumulate within days. It must be taken lifelong. Research into gene therapy approaches that could provide a lasting cure is ongoing.

faqs.question: What happens to adults with cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Adults with well-treated nephropathic cystinosis can maintain good kidney function for decades. However, cystine accumulates in muscles, causing progressive weakness and difficulty swallowing, in the thyroid (hypothyroidism), and in the brain. Regular multidisciplinary follow-up for these late complications is essential in adulthood.

faqs.question: How common is cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Cystinosis affects roughly 1 in 100,000 to 200,000 live births worldwide. About 2,000 patients have been identified in the United States. It is more common in populations of Northern European ancestry due to a founder mutation.

faqs.question: Does growth hormone help children with cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Recombinant human growth hormone is used in children with cystinosis who remain short despite optimised metabolic treatment. It typically improves height velocity by 2–4 cm per year and can partially correct growth deficit, though it does not address the underlying metabolic disorder.

faqs.question: Why does cystinosis cause rickets?
faqs.answer: Renal tubule damage causes excessive phosphate loss in the urine. Chronically low blood phosphate impairs the mineralisation of growing bone, producing the bone-softening disorder called rickets. Oral phosphate supplementation and vitamin D are given to prevent and treat this complication.

faqs.question: How is cystinosis monitored?
faqs.answer: Leukocyte cystine levels are measured every 3–6 months to verify that cysteamine is keeping cystine below the target of 1 nmol half-cystine per mg protein. Kidney function, thyroid tests, ophthalmology examinations, and growth parameters are reviewed at regular specialist visits.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the nephropathic and ocular forms of cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Nephropathic cystinosis affects virtually every organ and causes kidney failure in childhood without treatment. The ocular form (non-nephropathic) is limited mainly to corneal crystal deposits and photophobia; kidney function is normal throughout life. The ocular form is caused by different CTNS variants with milder effects on cystinosin function.

faqs.question: Can someone with cystinosis have children?
faqs.answer: Many adults with treated cystinosis have had children. All children of an affected parent are obligate carriers of one CTNS mutation. If the partner is also a carrier (roughly 1 in 150–200 in European populations), there is a 50% chance each child will be a carrier and a 25% chance of being affected. Genetic counselling before conception is recommended.

faqs.question: Why does cysteamine smell so bad?
faqs.answer: Cysteamine is a sulphur-containing compound that produces a strong sulphurous odour similar to rotten eggs. This is a well-known side effect that affects adherence. The delayed-release formulation (Procysbi) has somewhat less odour impact and also reduces dosing from four to two times daily, improving tolerability.

faqs.question: Is cystinosis the same as kidney stones?
faqs.answer: No. Kidney stones in cystinuria result from high urinary cystine excretion forming stones in the collecting system; leukocyte cystine levels are normal. Cystinosis involves cystine crystals trapped inside lysosomes throughout the body, with a very different mechanism and treatment.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with cystinosis?
faqs.answer: With modern cysteamine therapy and kidney transplantation, life expectancy for nephropathic cystinosis has improved substantially. Most patients now survive well into adulthood, with increasing numbers reaching their 40s and 50s. The main threats are late myopathic and neurological complications in adults over 30.

faqs.question: Are there clinical trials for cystinosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Research into gene therapy, stem cell transplantation, and novel cystine-depleting molecules is ongoing at several major centres. Patients can search clinicaltrials.gov using the term 'cystinosis' to find currently recruiting studies.

metaDescription: Cystinosis is a rare inherited disorder causing cystine crystal buildup in kidneys and other organs. Learn symptoms, cysteamine treatment, and prognosis.
